writing_task: I need to email a key customer — Priya at Northfield Logistics — letting her know that a feature she requested six months ago is finally shipping next week. She'll need to coordinate with her IT team to enable it, so there's a clear action item on her end.

drafts.rubric:
Subject: The reporting feature you requested is shipping next week

Hi Priya,

You flagged the advanced reporting feature back in the spring, and I know it's been a long wait since then. I wanted to make sure you heard it from me directly: it's shipping next week.

To actually turn it on, your IT team will need to enable it through the admin console — it won't activate automatically. Given that it touches your data pipeline, they'll probably want a day or two to review the settings before flipping it on.

Does it make sense to get a quick call on the calendar so I can walk them through what to expect?
drafts.generic:
Subject: Your Requested Feature Is Shipping Next Week

Hi Priya,

Great news — the feature you requested six months ago is finally here. We're officially shipping it next week, and I wanted to make sure you're ready to take full advantage of it from day one.

There is one action item on your end: your IT team will need to enable the feature within your account settings before it becomes active. I'd recommend looping them in this week so everything is ready to go on launch day.

Please let me know if you have any questions or need guidance for your IT team. Excited for you to finally have this!

Best,
[Your Name]
drafts.preference:
Subject: The feature you requested is shipping next week

Hi Priya,

Good news — the feature you requested back in the spring is finally ready and rolling out next week.

To turn it on, your IT team will need to enable it from the admin settings panel. It shouldn't take more than a few minutes once they're in there, but I want to make sure you have plenty of lead time to coordinate with them.

Can you confirm by Thursday whether your team is set to activate it on launch day? Happy to jump on a quick call if anything needs clarifying.

Thanks,
[Your name]

user_reason: B leads with acknowledgment of Priya's wait (reader-first framing), delivers the news cleanly, and the 'it won't activate automatically' plus the data pipeline note explains why this matters from her team's perspective — not ours. The ask is clear even if the deadline could be sharper. A is solid and direct but the 'I want to make sure you have plenty of lead time' centers the sender's reasoning, and 'Happy to jump on a quick call' after the CTA is trailing softness. Still, A beats C easily. C is the worst: 'take full advantage from day one' is filler, 'Excited for you to finally have this!' is exactly the kind of trailing sentiment that weakens the close, and 'Please let me know if you have any questions' is a non-action that diffuses the ask.

criteria_analysis.name: Emotional Sequencing
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft B opens by acknowledging Priya's original request and the long wait before delivering the news, earning the headline rather than leading with it — this mirrors exactly the sequencing pattern the user refined in conversation. Drafts A and C lead with 'good news' or 'great news' and deliver the headline immediately, with context trailing after rather than framing the reception.

criteria_analysis.name: Reader-Centered Framing
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft B names Priya's concrete situation — 'it touches your data pipeline' and acknowledges the wait from her perspective — speaking to her reality rather than explaining the sender's delivery. Drafts A and C stay generic ('the feature you requested'), and Draft C's 'take full advantage of it from day one' is aspirational framing centered on the sender's excitement rather than Priya's operational reality.

criteria_analysis.name: Forward-Momentum Closings
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft B closes with a concrete, actionable invitation — a call to walk the IT team through what to expect — putting the ball in Priya's court cleanly. Draft A asks for confirmation by Thursday, which is forward-moving but the 'Happy to jump on a quick call' feels like padding. Draft C ends on 'Excited for you to finally have this!' which is exactly the sentimental/motivational filler the user consistently flags as a problem.

criteria_analysis.name: Concrete Specificity
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft B names a tangible detail — 'it touches your data pipeline' and 'they'll probably want a day or two to review the settings' — giving Priya real texture about what's involved. Drafts A and C keep the action item abstract ('enable it from the admin settings panel' / 'enable the feature within your account settings') without naming what's actually at stake or what the IT team will encounter.

criteria_analysis.name: Concise Consolidation
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Drafts A and B are both lean and efficient — each paragraph serves a clear purpose without padding. Draft C over-specifies with phrases like 'take full advantage of it from day one' and 'I'd recommend looping them in this week so everything is ready to go on launch day,' which add padding where a consolidated statement would suffice.

criteria_analysis.name: Human Voice
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft B reads most naturally — 'I wanted to make sure you heard it from me directly' and 'it won't activate automatically' feel like a real person finishing a thought. Draft A is conversational but slightly more template-like. Draft C uses corporate-adjacent language ('take full advantage,' 'Excited for you to finally have this!') and a more formal cadence that distances it from genuine human voice.

per_criterion.reasoning: Draft B opens by acknowledging Priya's original request and the long wait before delivering the news, earning the headline rather than leading with it — this mirrors exactly the sequencing pattern the user refined in conversation. rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft B best embodies the user's core priorities: it sequences acknowledgment before the news, frames everything around Priya's concrete situation (data pipeline, IT review time), and closes on a clean, forward-moving action without sentimental filler. Draft A is solid and clean but lacks the specificity and emotional sequencing refinement. Draft C falls to last due to motivational closing language and sender-centered framing that directly conflict with the user's demonstrated preferences.

per_criterion.reasoning: Draft A is more specific and precise — it names the feature ('advanced reporting feature'), references when it was requested ('back in the spring'), and specifies where IT needs to act ('admin console'). Draft B is clear but more generic ('the feature you requested,' 'account settings').
Both drafts follow a logical flow: announcement, action needed, next step. Ideas connect smoothly in each.
Both drafts are free of grammatical errors and typos, with proper punctuation and sentence construction throughout.
Both are well-organized with a clear three-part structure: context/announcement, action item, and call to action. Paragraph breaks are appropriately placed in both.

Draft A feels more personal and conversational ('I know it's been a long wait,' 'heard it from me directly'), which is more engaging. Draft B's enthusiasm ('Great news,' 'Excited for you') feels slightly more formulaic and sales-templated.

Draft A strikes a natural, professional-yet-human tone that reads like a real person wrote it. Draft B is competent but leans toward a polished marketing/customer-success template tone with phrases like 'take full advantage' and 'Excited for you to finally have this!'

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A reads as a more authentic, specific, and naturally conversational email. It provides more concrete details (feature name, admin console, data pipeline considerations) and avoids the slightly templated enthusiasm of Draft B, making it more credible and engaging as professional communication.

writing_task: Help me write an email to our head of sales letting him know we're killing a feature that his team has been demoing to prospects — we discovered it creates a security liability and can't ship it. He needs to update his team's pitch decks before the conference next Thursday.

drafts.rubric:
Subject: Feature removal before the conference

Hi [Name],

Your team has been pitching [feature name] to prospects, and I know it's been part of the demo flow. We're pulling it — we found a security issue that makes it unshippable.

That means any prospect who saw it in a deck is expecting something you won't be able to deliver. With the conference next Thursday, your team would walk in with outdated pitches unless this gets fixed beforehand.

Can you update the pitch decks by Tuesday?
drafts.generic:
Subject: Urgent: Feature Removal Before Thursday's Conference

Hi [Name],

I need to flag an urgent issue. We've discovered that the [feature name] your team has been demoing to prospects creates a security liability, and we've made the decision to pull it from our roadmap entirely. It will not be shipping.

This means your team's pitch decks need to be updated before the conference next Thursday. Please remove all references to this feature from any demos, slides, or prospect-facing materials as soon as possible.

I'm happy to jump on a quick call to walk through the details and answer any questions. Let me know.

Thanks,
[Your Name]
drafts.preference:
Subject: Removing [Feature Name] from Sales Demos — Action Needed Before Thursday

Hi [Name],

We're pulling [feature name] from the product. Our team found a security vulnerability that makes it unshippable, so it can't be part of any demos or pitches going forward.

I know your team has been actively using it with prospects, so I wanted to flag this as quickly as possible. With the conference next Thursday, you'll need to get updated pitch decks to your reps before then.

Can you confirm once the decks are updated? Happy to jump on a quick call if it helps.

Thanks,
[Your name]

user_reason: C is the strongest — it follows the right emotional arc naturally: acknowledges the situation first, delivers the news, then frames the stakes from my reader's perspective ('prospect who saw it is expecting something you won't deliver' — that's his problem, not mine), and ends with a single clear owner and a real deadline. That's exactly how this should land. A is decent and gets the CTA right, but it buries the stakes and the 'why' framing is weak — 'I wanted to flag this quickly' centers me, not him. The paragraph order also feels slightly off; the urgency comes after the news instead of being embedded in the consequence. B is the weakest — 'I need to flag an urgent issue' is throat-clearing, the subject line screams panic, and 'please remove all references from any demos, slides, or prospect-facing materials' is a vague group ask with no deadline. It also closes with 'let me know' after a call offer, which trails off instead of driving action.

criteria_analysis.name: Emotional Sequencing
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft C follows the ideal sequence: acknowledges the reader's situation first ('Your team has been pitching [feature name]…'), then delivers the news, then explains why it matters to the reader (prospects expecting something undeliverable, walking into the conference with outdated pitches), then closes with one concrete next step. Draft A acknowledges the reader's situation but only after delivering the news in the opening line, making the acknowledgment feel retroactive. Draft B leads with 'I need to flag an urgent issue' — sender-centered framing — and the acknowledgment of the reader's situation is buried mid-sentence rather than appearing as a distinct opening beat.

criteria_analysis.name: Reader-Centered Framing
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft C excels here: it names the specific downstream consequence for the reader — prospects who saw the feature are now expecting something that won't be delivered, and the team would walk into the conference with outdated pitches. That's concrete, tangible, and about the reader's reality. Drafts A and B both mention the conference deadline but never name the actual cost to the reader; they explain what happened and what needs to change without framing why it matters in terms of the reader's world.

criteria_analysis.name: Human Voice
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft C reads like a sharp colleague finishing a thought — natural, direct, no corporate padding. Draft A is mostly natural but 'Happy to jump on a quick call if it helps' is slightly canned. Draft B uses phrases like 'flag an urgent issue,' 'we've made the decision,' and 'prospect-facing materials' which edge toward institutional/corporate tone.

criteria_analysis.name: Conciseness and Directness
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft C is the leanest — every sentence earns its place, no filler, no over-explanation. Draft A adds an unnecessary 'Happy to jump on a quick call if it helps' and a trailing 'Thanks.' Draft B over-specifies by listing out 'demos, slides, or prospect-facing materials' which breaks out what could be consolidated, and the offer to walk through details adds weight without value.

criteria_analysis.name: Forward-Momentum Closings
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft C ends perfectly on the ask with a specific deadline ('Can you update the pitch decks by Tuesday?') and stops — nothing after it. This is exactly what the user demanded in conversation history. Draft A adds 'Happy to jump on a quick call if it helps' and 'Thanks' after the CTA, diluting it. Draft B is the worst offender: after the ask ('Please remove all references…'), it adds an offer to jump on a call and 'Let me know' and 'Thanks' — three trailing elements after what should be the stopping point.

criteria_analysis.name: Action Ownership
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft C names the reader as owner ('Can you update the pitch decks') with a concrete deadline ('by Tuesday'). Drafts A and B both address the reader but lack a specific deadline — A says 'before then' (vague relative to Thursday) and asks for confirmation rather than setting a deadline, B says 'as soon as possible' which is not a concrete deadline. None of the drafts name a specific individual beyond the reader (which the user's rubric prefers), but Draft C comes closest to clean ownership with a hard date.

per_criterion.reasoning: Draft C follows the ideal sequence: acknowledges the reader's situation first ('Your team has been pitching [feature name]…'), then delivers the news, then explains why it matters to the reader (prospects expecting something undeliverable, walking into the conference with outdated pitches), then closes with one concrete next step. Draft A acknowledges the reader's situation but only after delivering the news in the opening line, making the acknowledgment feel retroactive. Draft B leads with 'I need to flag an urgent issue' — sender-centered framing — and the acknowledgment of the reader's situation is buried mid-sentence rather than appearing as a distinct opening beat.

That's concrete, tangible, and about the reader's reality. Drafts A and B both mention the conference deadline but never name the actual cost to the reader; they explain what happened and what needs to change without framing why it matters in terms of the reader's world.

Draft C reads like a sharp colleague finishing a thought — natural, direct, no corporate padding. Draft A is mostly natural but 'Happy to jump on a quick call if it helps' is slightly canned. Draft B uses phrases like 'flag an urgent issue,' 'we've made the decision,' and 'prospect-facing materials' which edge toward institutional/corporate tone.

Draft C is the leanest — every sentence earns its place, no filler, no over-explanation. Draft A adds an unnecessary 'Happy to jump on a quick call if it helps' and a trailing 'Thanks.' Draft B over-specifies by listing out 'demos, slides, or prospect-facing materials' which breaks out what could be consolidated, and the offer to walk through details adds weight without value.

Draft A adds 'Happy to jump on a quick call if it helps' and 'Thanks' after the CTA, diluting it. Draft B is the worst offender: after the ask ('Please remove all references…'), it adds an offer to jump on a call and 'Let me know' and 'Thanks' — three trailing elements after what should be the stopping point.

Drafts A and B both address the reader but lack a specific deadline — A says 'before then' (vague relative to Thursday) and asks for confirmation rather than setting a deadline, B says 'as soon as possible' which is not a concrete deadline. None of the drafts name a specific individual beyond the reader (which the user's rubric prefers), but Draft C comes closest to clean ownership with a hard date.

rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft C aligns most closely with the user's demonstrated preferences: it follows the exact emotional sequence (acknowledge → news → reader-centered impact → clean ask), names concrete downstream consequences for the reader's team, and ends on a specific action with a deadline — then stops. This mirrors the pattern the user praised in message #33. Draft A is decent but trails off with sentiment and lacks reader-centered framing. Draft B reads the most institutional and violates the closing principle most severely.

per_criterion.reasoning: Both are clear, but Draft B is more explicit about the scope of what needs to change ('demos, slides, or prospect-facing materials') and leaves no ambiguity about the decision being final ('It will not be shipping').
Both flow logically from problem to impact to action. Draft B's structure is slightly tighter, moving from issue to decision to ask to offer of support in a natural progression.
Both drafts are grammatically correct with no errors or typos.
Draft B has a clearer subject line flagging urgency, a more complete email structure (greeting, body, offer to help, sign-off), and better delineation of information versus action items.
Draft A has a slightly more conversational, punchy style that draws the reader in, while Draft B compensates with urgency signaling and a collaborative offer. Both are engaging for a business email.

Draft A's tone is direct and effective but slightly blunt ('We're pulling it') without offering collaboration. Draft B strikes a better balance between urgency and professionalism by offering a follow-up call, which is more appropriate for cross-team communication.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft B is the stronger email overall: it signals urgency in the subject line, is more explicit about scope and finality, follows complete email conventions with a sign-off, and offers collaborative support — all of which make it more professional and actionable for a high-stakes internal communication.

writing_task: I need to email our engineering and design leads about a scope change on the dashboard redesign — the CEO wants us to add real-time alerts, which means the timeline shifts and one of the teams needs to own the new component. Help me draft something that gets both leads aligned without micromanaging who does what.

drafts.rubric:
Subject: Dashboard Redesign — Scope Change + Timeline Impact

Hi both,

You've put real work into the current dashboard plan, and I want to loop you in before anything shifts under your feet.

The CEO has asked us to add real-time alerts to the redesign. That means the timeline moves, and one of your teams will need to own the alerts component going forward.

This affects your current sprint commitments and how you're resourcing the next phase — so the sooner we get aligned, the less rework lands on either of you.

Can the two of you connect by end of Thursday and come back to me with a proposed owner and a revised timeline?
drafts.generic:
Subject: Dashboard Redesign – Scope Update and Timeline Shift

Hi both,

Following a request from the CEO, we're adding a real-time alerts component to the dashboard redesign. This expands the project scope and will require us to adjust our timeline accordingly — I'll share revised dates once we've had a chance to align.

The alerts component will need a clear owner between engineering and design. I'd love for you two to connect directly and figure out how ownership and collaboration make the most sense given your current bandwidth.

Can we get on a quick call this week to sync? Happy to facilitate if useful.

Thanks
drafts.preference:
Subject: Dashboard Redesign — Scope Change + Timeline Update

Hey both,

The CEO has asked us to add real-time alerts to the dashboard redesign. It's a meaningful addition, so the timeline needs to shift, and one of our teams will need to own this new component.

I'd rather not prescribe how we split it — you two know the workloads and capacity better than I do. What I do need is alignment on ownership and a revised delivery estimate.

Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar this week to sort it out? Happy to set that up if you send me your availability.

Thanks

user_reason: A is the only one that gets the emotional arc right — it acknowledges the situation first, delivers the news, then frames the stakes from their perspective (sprint commitments, resourcing, rework landing on them), and closes with one concrete ask that has a real deadline. That closing line is forward-looking without any trailing sentiment. B is decent and direct but the ask is weak — 'send me your availability' is a soft group action with no deadline, and the tone drifts slightly sender-centric ('I'd rather not prescribe'). C is the worst: it buries the stakes entirely, the 'I'd love for you two to connect' is passive and vague, and 'Happy to facilitate if useful' is a non-action that trails off after the CTA. C also has a whiff of corporate hedging that I don't love.

criteria_analysis.name: Emotional Sequencing
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft A nails the full sequence: acknowledgment ('You've put real work into the current dashboard plan') as a standalone beat, then the news, then why it matters to the readers (sprint commitments, resourcing), then one ask. Draft B skips acknowledgment entirely and jumps straight to the news — a clear violation the user has flagged as unacceptable. Draft C opens with a framing sentence but it's situation-framing ('Following a request from the CEO'), not acknowledgment of the readers' effort or situation — and the news and context are slightly collapsed.

criteria_analysis.name: Reader-Centered Framing
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft A explicitly names the readers' concrete reality — 'your current sprint commitments' and 'how you're resourcing the next phase' — and ties impact to rework landing on them. Draft B's 'why it matters' is essentially 'you know your workloads better than I do,' which flatters but doesn't name concrete impact; it doesn't earn the ask. Draft C says 'given your current bandwidth' but doesn't articulate what specifically is at stake for the readers — it stays abstract and organizational.

criteria_analysis.name: Action Ownership
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft A assigns ownership to 'the two of you,' gives a concrete deadline ('end of Thursday'), and specifies deliverables (proposed owner + revised timeline). Draft B asks for a meeting 'this week' — vaguer timeframe — and puts the scheduling burden ambiguously ('Happy to set that up if you send me your availability'). Draft C is the weakest: 'Can we get on a quick call this week' is a vague group ask with no deadline and no specified deliverable.

criteria_analysis.name: Conciseness and Directness
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft A is lean — every sentence earns its place, no over-explanation, short paragraphs. Draft B is nearly as tight but the 'I'd rather not prescribe how we split it' line is slightly unnecessary — the user asked not to micromanage, but spelling out that you're not micromanaging is a mild over-explanation. Draft C has padding: 'This expands the project scope and will require us to adjust our timeline accordingly — I'll share revised dates once we've had a chance to align' explains what the reader can infer, and 'Happy to facilitate if useful' is filler.

criteria_analysis.name: Forward-Momentum Closings
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft A ends cleanly on the ask with no trailing sentiment or justification. Draft B adds 'Thanks' after the CTA, which is minor trailing sentiment. Draft C has two issues: 'Happy to facilitate if useful' is a trailing hedge after the ask, and 'Thanks' follows — both dilute the CTA.

criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft A reads like a sharp colleague — 'shifts under your feet,' 'the less rework lands on either of you' — natural, direct, no jargon. Draft B is conversational and warm but 'I'd rather not prescribe' has a slightly managerial cadence. Draft C leans more formal/corporate: 'Following a request from the CEO,' 'I'd love for you two to connect directly' — reads more institutional than personal.

Draft A has a clean four-paragraph structure with a strong subject line that previews both scope and timeline impact. Draft B is well-organized but the subject line is slightly less informative (no hint of action needed), and the closing ask is softer and less structured.

Draft B is appropriately professional but slightly more distant and formulaic; 'I'd love for you two to connect' feels a touch overly soft for a directive.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A is the stronger email: it opens with empathy, communicates the change and its impact with precision, and closes with a specific, time-bound ask. Draft B is solid and professional but is less direct in its call-to-action and less engaging in its tone, making it slightly less effective as an internal communication that needs to drive action.
